title: Strings: Working with Text 📝
description: Create, combine, and explore text data in JavaScript.

slides.title: What is a String? 🧶
slides.content:
# Text Data in JavaScript

A **String** is simply a sequence of characters (letters, numbers, spaces, emojis) wrapped in quotes. 

We saw them in Lesson 1, but let's review:

```javascript
let firstName = "Alice";      // Double quotes
let lastName = 'Smith';       // Single quotes
let quote = `Code is poetry`; // Backticks
```

Strings track text exactly as you type it — spaces and all!

slides.title: Adding Strings Together ➕
slides.content:
## String Concatenation

What happens if you use the `+` operator on strings instead of numbers?

It **concatenates** (glues) them together end-to-end!

```javascript
let greeting = "Hello" + "World"; // "HelloWorld" (No space!)
```

Notice that JavaScript doesn't add spaces automatically when you concatenate. You have to add them yourself:

```javascript
let greeting = "Hello" + " " + "World"; // "Hello World"

let first = "Bob";
let last = "Ross";
let fullName = first + " " + last; // "Bob Ross"
```

quizData.question: What is the output of: "Super" + "man"?
quizData.explanation: The + operator glues strings together exactly as they are. Since there is no space specified, it forms "Superman".

slides.title: Template Literals: The Modern Way ✨
Template Literals in Action 💻
slides.content: Notice how much cleaner the backtick method is compared to the plus signs:

quizData.question: Which characters are used to create a Template Literal that allows ${} variable injection?
quizData.explanation: Only backticks (``) allow you to use Template Literals and inject variables using ${varName}.

slides.title: Types Colliding! 💥
slides.content:
## String + Number = ?

What happens if you try to add a String and a Number together?

```javascript
let result = "Apple" + 5;
```

Instead of crashing, JavaScript aggressively converts the Number into a String, and concatenates them!

`"Apple" + 5` becomes `"Apple5"`

This is called **Type Coercion**. JavaScript tries to be helpful by guessing what you want, but this can lead to sneaky bugs:

```javascript
let math = "5" + 5;
// Output is "55", NOT 10! The string "5" forced the number 5 to become a string.
```

quizData.question: What is the result of letting x = "10" + 20;
quizData.explanation: Because "10" is a String, the + operator behaves like glue. It converts 20 to a string and sticks them together to make "1020".
